finding: Congruence between momentary affective concepts and embodied affective reactions enhances cognitive performance and processing efficiency. Incongruence produces measurable deficits and heightens negative affect, suggesting that matching affective systems creates epistemically functional fluency.

Global information processing promotes inclusion and assimilation to standards, while local processing promotes exclusion and contrast. These effects persist across multiple induction methods and override scale-type effects, indicating processing style as a fundamental moderator of social judgment.

Communicated anger in bargaining signals high limits to opponents, but behavioral outcomes depend on structural factors. Deception-enabling structures reverse the anger advantage by allowing communicators to misinform opponents and lower their perceived limits, while low-power contexts eliminate anger's effectiveness.

People project their own responsiveness onto partners, perceiving reciprocal care regardless of actual partner responsiveness. This projection predicts relationship promotion behaviors and partially mediates the link between own caring and commitment, suggesting projection serves an adaptive confidence-building function.

Threats to personal control increase endorsement of external systems of control (religious and governmental) in a compensatory fashion. This effect is mediated by defensive reactions against randomness and moderated by perceived system benevolence, holding across 67 nations.

Moral rebels are resented by individuals whose behavior they implicitly condemn, but admired by uninvolved observers. Rejection is mediated by imagined reproach threatening moral self-worth and is eliminated when actors are self-affirmed, showing resentment functions to defend self-adequacy.
